_meta.disclaimer: All chemical dosages sourced from IITA TAAT Compacts, NAFDAC-registered product guides, and Nigerian Federal Ministry of Agriculture extension manuals.
_meta.low_confidence_response: The image is unclear or the symptoms are not distinct enough for a reliable diagnosis. Please retake the photo in good natural light, focusing closely on the most affected leaf. If symptoms persist, contact your nearest agricultural extension officer.

visual_markers: Dark brown circular spots with concentric bullseye rings on leaves
Yellow halo surrounding each spot
Spots start on oldest lower leaves and progress upward
Spots 5–15mm diameter
Infected leaves turn yellow-brown and drop prematurely
Stem lesions are oval-shaped with same concentric ring pattern

severity.severe: Most foliage defoliated; stem and fruit lesions present

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy infected leaves — do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply copper sulphate Bordeaux mixture (1%) every 7–10 days.
organic_treatment.step_3: Improve spacing to 50cm between plants, 75cm between rows.
organic_treatment.step_4: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash.
organic_treatment.step_5: Avoid overhead irrigation; water at base in the morning.
organic_treatment.source: IITA Tomato IPM Field Guide, TAAT Vegetable Compact 2022

chemical_treatment.dosage: Mancozeb: 2g per litre. Chlorothalonil: 2.5g per litre.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 7–14 days from first symptom. Stop 7 days before harvest.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and nose mask. Observe 7-day PHI for Mancozeb, 14 days for Chlorothalonil.

prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds or resistant varieties (e.g., Tropimech, UC-82)
Practice 2–3 year crop rotation
Remove plant debris after harvest
Apply preventive copper spray at transplanting
tomato_early_blight.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 30% of crop is severely affected after 2 weeks of treatment, contact your extension officer.

visual_markers: Large irregular water-soaked grey-green lesions that quickly turn brown-black
White fluffy mould on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Spreads extremely fast — whole leaf destroyed in 2–3 days
Dark brown lesions on stems with white mould
Fruit lesions are firm, brown, greasy
Affected tissue smells foul

severity.mild: Few water-soaked spots on one or two leaves
severity.moderate: Multiple plants affected; stems showing lesions
severity.severe: Entire field collapsing; irreversible crop loss

organic_treatment.step_1: URGENT: Remove and burn all infected plants immediately — late blight spreads in days.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply copper Bordeaux mixture every 5–7 days.
organic_treatment.step_4: Switch to drip or furrow irrigation — avoid wetting foliage.
organic_treatment.step_5: Destroy volunteer tomato and potato plants nearby.
organic_treatment.source: IITA Tomato Disease Management Manual, FAO Plant Production Paper

chemical_treatment.product_name: Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) or Cymoxanil + Mancozeb
chemical_treatment.common_brand_in_nigeria: Ridomil Gold MZ 68 WP, Curzate M8
chemical_treatment.dosage: Ridomil Gold MZ: 2.5g per litre. Curzate M8: 3g per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Full protective gear required. Observe 7-day PHI. Do not use Metalaxyl more than 3 times per season.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC 2023, IITA Late Blight Management Protocol

prevention: Plant resistant varieties: Cal-J, Fla-7775
Avoid planting near potato fields
Begin preventive sprays before rainy season
tomato_late_blight.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Late blight is a crop emergency. If you see rapid brown-black spread and foul smell, contact your extension officer IMMEDIATELY.

visual_markers: Serpentine white mines (winding galleries) inside leaves
Blotchy silvery patches where larvae ate leaf mesophyll
Small holes in leaves and fruit
Frass visible inside mines when held to light
Curled or distorted young terminal leaves

severity.moderate: Multiple leaves and some fruit damaged
severity.severe: Heavy defoliation; extensive fruit damage with secondary rotting

organic_treatment.step_2: Remove and destroy severely mined leaves.
organic_treatment.step_3: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray every 5–7 days.
organic_treatment.step_5: Install pheromone traps for adult males.
organic_treatment.source: IITA Tuta absoluta West Africa Guide, FAO IPM Bulletin 2021

chemical_treatment.product_name: Spinosad 48% SC or Emamectin Benzoate 5% SG
chemical_treatment.dosage: Spinosad: 0.3ml per litre. Emamectin: 0.2g per litre.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 7 days, rotating chemical groups. Max 3 applications per group per season.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Spinosad: 1-day PHI. Emamectin: 3 days. Highly toxic to bees — do NOT spray during flowering.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC 2023, IITA Tuta absoluta Chemical Protocol

prevention: Use 0.8mm mesh netting over nursery seedbeds
Destroy crop residues after harvest
Rotate with non-solanaceous crops
tomato_leaf_miner.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 50% of fruits are damaged or infestation persists after 3 weeks, contact your extension officer.

visual_markers: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to grey-brown velvety mould on underside of leaf
Leaves curl inward and turn yellow
Primarily affects upper canopy

severity.mild: Few yellow spots on upper leaves

organic_treatment.step_1: Prune lower leaves to increase airflow.
organic_treatment.step_3: Apply copper Bordeaux mixture every 7–10 days.

chemical_treatment.dosage: Azoxystrobin: 1ml per litre. Chlorothalonil: 2.5g per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. 3-day PHI for Azoxystrobin, 14 days for Chlorothalonil.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties where available
Ensure wide plant spacing
Avoid excessive nitrogen
tomato_leaf_mold.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If symptoms persist after 2 weeks of treatment or more than half the canopy is affected.

visual_markers: Small water-soaked circular spots (2–3mm) turning dark brown with yellow halo
Spots have angular edges limited by leaf veins
Spots coalesce causing large necrotic areas
Small raised scabby lesions on fruit
Worst in warm, rainy, or humid conditions

severity.mild: Few small spots on lower leaves
severity.moderate: Multiple leaves affected; some fruit scabbing

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy infected plant material.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply copper hydroxide spray every 5–7 days in wet weather.
organic_treatment.step_4: Disinfect tools with 10% bleach between plants.

chemical_treatment.product_name: Copper Hydroxide 77% WP or Copper Oxychloride 50% WP
chemical_treatment.dosage: Copper Hydroxide: 2.5g per litre. Copper Oxychloride: 3g per litre.

prevention: Use certified pathogen-free seeds
Treat seeds with hot water (50°C, 25 min) before planting
Do not save seeds from infected fruit
tomato_bacterial_spot.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Bacterial spot is hard to distinguish from other bacterial diseases. If unsure or disease spreading rapidly, contact extension officer.

visual_markers: Numerous small (1–3mm) circular spots with dark borders and tan/white centres
Tiny black dots visible in spot centres (pycnidia)
Starts on oldest lower leaves — very high spot density
Heavy spotting causes yellowing and leaf drop

severity.mild: Few spots on lower leaves only
severity.moderate: Lower half severely spotted and defoliating

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove infected lower leaves and destroy.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply copper Bordeaux mixture every 7–10 days.
organic_treatment.step_3: Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash.

chemical_treatment.dosage: Mancozeb: 2g per litre. Azoxystrobin: 1ml per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. 7-day PHI for Mancozeb, 3 days for Azoxystrobin.

prevention: Rotate crops — no tomato in same bed for 2+ years
Use drip irrigation
Remove plant debris after harvest
tomato_septoria_leaf_spot.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If defoliation progresses faster than treatment can control, or more than 75% of foliage is lost.

visual_markers: Brown circular spots with concentric rings (bullseye) — larger than early blight (up to 25mm)
Irregular dark brown edges with lighter brown centres
Yellow halo around spots
Sunken dark circular lesions with rings on fruit

severity.mild: Few spots on lower or mid-plant leaves
severity.moderate: Multiple leaves and some fruit affected

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruit.
organic_treatment.step_3: Improve airflow — prune and correct spacing.

chemical_treatment.dosage: Azoxystrobin: 1ml per litre. Difenoconazole: 0.5ml per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. 3-day PHI for Azoxystrobin.

prevention: Use disease-resistant varieties
3-year crop rotation
Remove all crop debris after harvest
tomato_target_spot.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Target spot is easily confused with early blight. If uncertain, consult your extension officer.

tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.pathogen: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (Begomovirus) — spread by whitefly Bemisia tabaci
visual_markers: Leaves curl upward and inward especially young leaves
Leaves turn yellow at margins and between veins
Leaves become small, thick, and leathery
Stunted plant growth
Flowers drop without setting fruit
Tiny white whiteflies on underside of leaves

severity.mild: Few leaves curling; plant still flowering
severity.moderate: Most new growth affected; flower drop beginning
severity.severe: Entire plant stunted; no fruit production

tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.critical_note: TYLCV has NO chemical cure. Once infected, the plant cannot be cured. Remove infected plants and control the whitefly vector.

organic_treatment.step_1: IMMEDIATELY remove and destroy all infected plants — burn, do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and trap adult whiteflies.
organic_treatment.step_4: Use reflective silver mulch to disorient whitefly adults.
organic_treatment.step_5: Plant trap crops (nasturtium) around the field.
organic_treatment.source: IITA TYLCV Management Guide, FAO Whitefly-Transmitted Viruses Bulletin 2022

chemical_treatment.product_name: Imidacloprid 70% WG or Thiamethoxam 25% WG (for whitefly control)
chemical_treatment.dosage: Imidacloprid: 0.5g per litre for foliar OR soil drench at transplanting.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 7–10 days for foliar. Rotate with non-neonicotinoid insecticides.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Observe 7-day PHI. Harmful to bees — do NOT apply during flowering.

prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant varieties: TY Zorro F1, Tyler F1 (TY-resistant hybrids)
Use certified disease-free transplants — inspect seedlings before planting
Install 50-mesh insect-proof netting over seedbeds
Rogue infected plants within first 3–4 weeks
Maintain 50m buffer zone between new and older infected fields
tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 20% of plants are infected or whitefly populations not responding to treatment, contact extension officer immediately.

visual_markers: Tiny yellow/white stippling (pin-prick dots) on upper leaf surface
Leaves turn bronze, silvery, or yellow with speckled appearance
Fine silken webbing on underside of leaves and between leaves
Tiny oval mites (0.5mm) visible on underside — red, yellow or green

severity.mild: Light stippling on a few leaves
severity.moderate: Extensive stippling and bronzing; webbing visible
severity.severe: Heavy webbing covering plant; mass leaf drop

organic_treatment.step_1: Spray plants forcefully with water to knock mites off — do this in the morning.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply neem oil (1–2%) spray every 5 days.
organic_treatment.step_4: Maintain adequate soil moisture — mites thrive in drought.

chemical_treatment.dosage: Abamectin: 0.5ml per litre. Spiromesifen: 0.75ml per litre.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 7–10 days. ROTATE acaricide groups — mites develop resistance very fast.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves, mask, eye protection. Abamectin: 3-day PHI. Spiromesifen: 7 days.

prevention: Avoid water stress — irrigate adequately in dry season
Monitor weekly — mite populations double every 5–7 days
Keep field borders free of weeds
tomato_spider_mites.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If mite populations not suppressed after 2 weeks, resistance may have developed. Consult extension officer for alternative acaricides.

visual_markers: Deep green leaves with no spots, lesions, or yellowing
Smooth leaf texture with no distortion, curling, or mines
No insects, webbing, frass, or mould visible

organic_treatment.step_1: Your plant appears healthy — continue current management.
organic_treatment.step_2: Monitor weekly for early signs of disease or pests.
organic_treatment.step_3: Apply preventive copper spray at start of rainy season.

prevention: Continue weekly crop monitoring
Maintain balanced NPK fertilisation
Keep field weeds under control
tomato_healthy.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Your tomato appears healthy. Contact extension officer for routine agronomic advice.

visual_markers: Long elliptical (cigar-shaped) lesions 2.5–15cm on leaves
Lesions grey-green at first, turning tan/straw-coloured
Distinct pale centres with darker wavy edges
Dark olive-green to black spores in humid conditions
Starts on lower leaves and progresses upward

severity.severe: Lesions on upper leaves; more than 50% leaf area destroyed

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy heavily infected lower leaves early — do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Ensure adequate spacing (75cm rows, 25–30cm within row) to maximise airflow.
organic_treatment.step_4: Practice crop rotation — no maize in same plot more than once every 2 seasons.
organic_treatment.source: IITA TAAT Maize Compact 2022, NRCRI Umudike IPM Field Guide

chemical_treatment.dosage: Mancozeb: 2.5g per litre. Propiconazole: 0.5ml per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. 14-day PHI for Propiconazole.

prevention: Plant resistant varieties: SAMMAZ 15, SAMMAZ 23, EVDT 99
2-year crop rotation
Balanced NPK fertilisation
Weed control
maize_northern_leaf_blight.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 50% leaf area above the ear is affected before grain fill, contact extension officer to assess field viability.

visual_markers: Ragged irregular feeding holes in leaves — young caterpillars skeletonise leaves
Wet sawdust-like frass in the leaf whorl
Caterpillars (up to 4cm) with inverted Y marking on head
Whorl may appear wilted and dead with frass inside

severity.mild: Few plants with leaf holes and frass
severity.severe: More than 25% severely damaged; whorl destruction; cob penetration

organic_treatment.step_1: Apply fine sand or ash directly into the whorl to kill young larvae.
organic_treatment.step_2: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis var. kurstaki (Btk) into whorl every 5–7 days.
organic_treatment.step_3: Hand-pick and destroy large caterpillars early morning.
organic_treatment.step_4: Apply neem seed kernel extract (50g per litre, filtered) into the whorl.
organic_treatment.step_5: Intercrop with legumes to attract natural enemies.
organic_treatment.source: IITA Fall Armyworm Nigeria Management Guide, FAO FAW Africa Programme 2021

option_1.dosage: 0.2g per litre — apply directly into whorl
option_1.frequency: Every 7–10 days. Maximum 3 applications per season.
option_1.phe_warning: Observe 3-day PHI. Wear gloves and mask.

option_2.dosage: Chlorpyrifos: 2ml per litre. Lambda-cyhalothrin: 1ml per litre.
option_2.frequency: Every 7 days during severe infestation only. Rotate with Emamectin.
option_2.phe_warning: 14-day PHI for Chlorpyrifos. 7 days for Lambda-cyhalothrin. Highly toxic to bees. Full PPE required.

chemical_treatment.note: Apply into the whorl, not on the leaf surface. Treat when larvae are young — ineffective against large caterpillars.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC 2023, IITA FAW Chemical Control Recommendations

prevention: Plant early to escape peak FAW pressure
Scout fields 2–3 times per week from emergence
Destroy crop residues after harvest
maize_fall_armyworm.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 20% of plants severely damaged, or insecticide treatment not working within 10 days, contact extension officer. FAW develops resistance quickly.

cassava_mosaic_disease.pathogen: African cassava mosaic virus (ACMV) — transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci
visual_markers: Mosaic pattern of yellow and green patches on leaves
Leaves distorted, crinkled, or reduced in size
Leaflets may be missing sections
Stunted plant growth
White whiteflies on underside of young leaves

severity.mild: Light mosaic; growth not severely affected
severity.moderate: Clear mosaic; some leaf distortion; yield reduced 20–40%
severity.severe: Severe mosaic and stunting; yield loss greater than 80%

cassava_mosaic_disease.critical_note: CMD has NO chemical cure. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Plant only CMD-resistant varieties.

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove by roots and destroy all infected plants — burn or bury deeply.
organic_treatment.step_2: Use yellow sticky traps to capture whitefly adults.
organic_treatment.step_5: Source clean planting stems from disease-free stock.
organic_treatment.source: IITA CMD Management Guide, NRCRI Umudike Cassava Disease Bulletin 2022

chemical_treatment.product_name: Imidacloprid 70% WG or Thiamethoxam 25% WG (for whitefly vector control only)
chemical_treatment.dosage: Imidacloprid: 0.5g per litre. Thiamethoxam: 0.3g per litre.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 10–14 days on new plantings to protect from reinfection.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: 7-day PHI. Avoid during flowering. Rotate with non-neonicotinoids.

prevention: ALWAYS plant certified CMD-resistant varieties: TME 419, NR 8082, IITA TMS 30572, UMUCASS 36
Source planting stems from certified disease-free nurseries only
Rogue infected plants early before whitefly populations build up
Control weeds — they serve as alternative whitefly hosts

resistant_varieties.recommended: TME 419, NR 8082, IITA TMS 30572 — CMD-resistant and high-yielding.
resistant_varieties.where_to_obtain: Contact your State ADP or nearest IITA office for certified planting material.

cassava_mosaic_disease.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 10% of cassava shows CMD symptoms, contact extension officer immediately — CMD spreads rapidly.

visual_markers: Angular water-soaked leaf spots turning brown — limited by leaf veins
Wilting of leaves at shoot tips (shepherd's crook wilting)
Yellow halo around angular leaf spots
Gummosis — sticky yellow-brown ooze on cut stems
Elongated dark stem cankers
Brown vascular discolouration when stem cut crosswise

severity.moderate: Extensive spotting; some tip wilting; stem cankers

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy all infected plant parts — burn, do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Disinfect cutting tools in 10% bleach solution between plants.
organic_treatment.step_3: Apply copper oxychloride 50% WP every 7–10 days.
organic_treatment.step_4: Do not plant in waterlogged soils.
organic_treatment.step_5: Use only healthy certified stems for replanting.
organic_treatment.source: IITA Cassava Disease Management Guide, NRCRI CBB Control Bulletin

chemical_treatment.product_name: Copper Oxychloride 50% WP or Copper Hydroxide 77% WP
chemical_treatment.dosage: Copper Oxychloride: 3g per litre. Copper Hydroxide: 2.5g per litre.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. 1-day PHI. Do not apply in hot sun.

prevention: Plant CBB-tolerant varieties
Use only certified disease-free planting material
Avoid cutting plants when wet
Practice 2-year crop rotation
cassava_bacterial_blight.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If wilting and stem die-back spreading rapidly or bacterial ooze is extensive, contact extension officer. Severe CBB may require field abandonment.

visual_markers: Leaf margins turn pale green then yellow-white, starting from tip and edges
A wavy yellow-white band spreads down the leaf margin toward the base
Infected leaves may wilt and roll slightly
Bacterial ooze (milky droplets) visible on cut leaf edges in humid morning conditions
Entire leaf may die and turn straw-coloured in severe cases
Young seedlings show uniform yellowing and collapse (kresek phase)

severity.mild: Yellow margins on a few leaves; most of canopy still green
severity.moderate: 30-50% of leaves showing yellowing; some leaf death
severity.severe: Widespread leaf death; kresek phase on seedlings; significant yield loss

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy severely infected plant material — do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Drain fields if possible — reduce standing water which spreads bacteria.
organic_treatment.step_3: Avoid overhead irrigation or flooding during active infection.
organic_treatment.step_4: Apply copper-based spray: copper oxychloride 50% WP at 30g per 15-litre sprayer every 10-14 days.
organic_treatment.step_5: Disinfect farming tools with 10% bleach solution between fields.
organic_treatment.source: IRRI Rice Knowledge Bank; IITA Rice Production Guidelines Nigeria

chemical_treatment.product_name: Copper oxychloride 50% WP or Streptomycin sulphate
chemical_treatment.dosage: Copper oxychloride: 30g per 15-litre sprayer. Streptomycin: 15g per 15-litre.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 10-14 days from first symptoms. Begin at tillering stage.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. Copper compounds are toxic to fish — do not spray near streams. Streptomycin: observe 7-day PHI.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC registered product class. IRRI Disease Management Guidelines.

prevention: Plant certified disease-free seeds from NASC or state ADP
Use resistant or tolerant varieties: FARO 52, FARO 57, WITA 4 (available from NCRI Badeggi)
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertiliser — lush growth is more susceptible
Do not allow water to flow from infected to healthy fields
Control leaf-cutting insects — wounds are entry points for bacteria
rice_bacterial_blight.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If kresek (seedling collapse) is spreading or more than 30% of the crop is affected, contact your extension officer immediately — bacterial blight can spread rapidly in wet conditions.

visual_markers: Diamond-shaped or spindle-shaped lesions with grey-white centres and brown-red borders
Lesions have a distinctive pointed shape at both ends — like an eye
Lesions can coalesce and kill entire leaves
Neck blast: dark brown to black lesion at the base of the panicle — causes entire head to die (white head)
Node blast: dark brown or black lesions at nodes — weakens stem
In humid conditions grey-green spore mass may be visible in lesion centre

severity.mild: Few small lesions on lower leaves; no neck or node symptoms
severity.moderate: Multiple lesions on several leaves; some node involvement
severity.severe: Neck blast causing white heads (no grain fill); widespread leaf death

organic_treatment.step_1: Remove and destroy severely affected leaves and panicles — do not compost.
organic_treatment.step_2: Avoid excessive nitrogen (especially late applications) — blast thrives on lush growth.
organic_treatment.step_3: Reduce plant spacing to improve airflow and reduce leaf wetness periods.
organic_treatment.step_4: Silicon application: if silicon fertiliser is available, it strengthens leaf cell walls and reduces blast penetration.
organic_treatment.source: IRRI Rice Doctor; NCRI Badeggi Rice Disease Management Manual

chemical_treatment.frequency: Apply at first sign of disease and again 10-14 days later. Critical window: before heading (panicle initiation stage).
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. Observe 14-day PHI for tricyclazole. Do not mix with herbicides.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC registered product class. IRRI Blast Management Protocol.

prevention: Plant blast-resistant varieties: FARO 57, FARO 60, NERICA varieties — ask NCRI Badeggi or your ADP
Avoid planting in areas with history of blast during cool, wet, foggy weather
Do not apply heavy nitrogen doses late in the season
Time planting to avoid peak blast weather (cool nights, wet mornings)
Use balanced NPK — potassium strengthens cell walls
rice_blast.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Neck blast can destroy an entire crop within days of heading. If you see brown or black discolouration at the base of panicles, contact your extension officer immediately.

visual_markers: Oval to circular spots, 1-2cm wide, dark brown with a yellow halo on leaves
Spots have dark brown margins and lighter brown centres — sometimes with grey or white centres
Heavy spotting causes leaves to turn yellow and die prematurely
Grain spots: dark brown to black oval spots on rice grains — causes discolouration and quality loss
Seedling infection causes brown spots and death at early growth stages
Most severe on nutrient-deficient plants, especially low potassium and silicon

severity.mild: A few spots on lower leaves; plant vigour not significantly reduced
severity.moderate: Multiple leaves affected; some early leaf death; grain spots beginning
severity.severe: Widespread leaf death; heavily spotted grain; significant yield and quality loss

organic_treatment.step_1: Address soil nutrition first — brown spot is strongly associated with nutrient-deficient soils. Apply balanced NPK fertiliser.
organic_treatment.step_2: Potassium application is particularly important — apply muriate of potash (KCl) if deficiency is suspected.
organic_treatment.step_3: Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves to reduce spore load.
organic_treatment.step_4: Treat seeds before planting: soak seeds in hot water (52-55°C for 10-12 minutes) to reduce seed-borne inoculum.
organic_treatment.source: IRRI Rice Knowledge Bank; NCRI Badeggi Nigeria

chemical_treatment.frequency: Every 14 days from first symptom. Also apply as preventive seed treatment.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: Wear gloves and mask. Observe 7-day PHI for Mancozeb.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC registered product class. IRRI Disease Management.

prevention: Use certified, clean seeds — brown spot is seed-borne
Treat seeds before planting (hot water or seed fungicide dip)
Maintain good soil nutrition — particularly phosphorus and potassium
Avoid water stress and waterlogging — both increase susceptibility
Do not apply excessive nitrogen without matching K and P fertilisation
rice_brown_spot.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 30% of grain is spotted at harvest, quality loss will be significant. Contact your extension officer for soil testing and fertiliser recommendations.

rice_tungro.pathogen: Rice tungro bacilliform virus (RTBV) + Rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV) — transmitted by green leafhopper (Nephotettix virescens)
visual_markers: Leaves turn orange-yellow or yellow, starting from the tip and spreading downward
Stunted plant growth — infected plants are noticeably shorter than healthy plants nearby
Tillering is severely reduced — infected plants produce very few side shoots
Leaves may show rust-coloured speckling or streaking
Interveinal chlorosis: yellowing between the leaf veins
Green leafhoppers (small, 3-4mm, bright green insects) often visible on undersides of leaves

severity.mild: Yellow discolouration on a few plants; slight stunting
severity.moderate: Multiple plants yellowed and stunted; reduced tillering
severity.severe: Widespread yellowing and severe stunting; minimal or no grain production

rice_tungro.critical_note: Tungro has NO chemical cure. Once a plant is infected, the virus cannot be removed. Management focuses entirely on controlling the green leafhopper vector and removing infected plants to prevent spread.

organic_treatment.step_1: IMMEDIATELY rogue out (uproot and destroy) all visibly infected plants — burn or bury deeply. Do not leave them in or near the field.
organic_treatment.step_2: Control green leafhopper: place yellow sticky traps (2 per 100m²) to monitor and capture adults.
organic_treatment.step_3: Synchronise planting with neighbouring farmers to break the leafhopper migration cycle — staggered planting increases disease pressure.
organic_treatment.step_4: Remove weeds from field borders — many grasses and weeds are alternative hosts for both the virus and the leafhopper.
organic_treatment.step_5: Use light traps at night to mass-capture leafhopper adults during migration periods.
organic_treatment.source: IRRI Rice Knowledge Bank; NCRI Badeggi Tungro Management Guide

chemical_treatment.product_name: Imidacloprid 70% WG or Thiamethoxam 25% WG (for leafhopper vector control)
chemical_treatment.dosage: Imidacloprid: 0.5g per litre for foliar spray OR seed treatment at 3g per kg seed.
chemical_treatment.frequency: Seed treatment at planting gives 3-4 weeks protection. Foliar: every 10-14 days during peak leafhopper season. Rotate with non-neonicotinoid insecticides.
chemical_treatment.phe_warning: 7-day PHI. Avoid during flowering — highly toxic to bees and beneficial insects. Rotate with Buprofezin or Etofenprox to manage resistance.
chemical_treatment.source: NAFDAC registered product class. IRRI Leafhopper Control Protocol.

prevention: Plant tungro-resistant varieties: FARO 52, WITA 4, NERICA L-19, NERICA L-41 — available from NCRI Badeggi or state ADP
Use certified disease-free seed — never plant from infected fields
Synchronise planting in your area — agree with neighbours to plant within 2 weeks of each other
Rogue infected plants within the first 3-4 weeks before leafhopper populations build
Avoid replanting into fields with tungro history without fallowing or fumigation
rice_tungro.when_to_seek_extension_officer: If more than 10% of plants show tungro symptoms, or if leafhopper populations are not responding to insecticide treatment, contact your extension officer immediately. Tungro can spread to entire fields within one season.

visual_markers: Upright, deep green leaves with no spots, lesions, or discolouration
Uniform plant height and vigour across the field
No visible insects, webbing, or fungal growth on leaves or stems
Clear green colour from base to tip — no yellowing, browning, or orange discolouration

organic_treatment.step_1: Your rice plant appears healthy — continue current management.
organic_treatment.step_2: Monitor weekly, especially at tillering and heading stages when diseases are most damaging.
organic_treatment.step_3: Apply preventive copper spray at tillering if blast pressure is high in your area.
organic_treatment.source: IRRI Rice Production Guidelines; NCRI Badeggi Nigeria

prevention: Continue weekly crop monitoring — check both leaf and stem health
Maintain balanced NPK fertilisation — split applications for better uptake
Keep field borders weed-free to reduce insect pest and disease pressure
Ensure proper water management — avoid waterlogging and drought stress
rice_healthy.when_to_seek_extension_officer: Your rice appears healthy. Contact your extension officer for routine agronomic advice on fertiliser timing and irrigation scheduling for your specific rice variety.
